scenarios.chosen: SES-aware admission-equity regulation with coaching-resistant exam formats and uniform score thresholds across maintainers

rejected.framing: Admission-equity regulation through family-capital adjustment weighting in the existing exam format
rejected.reason: Rejected because adjusting exam weighting without redesigning the coaching-sensitivity of the format itself would preserve the structural advantage of high test-prep investment; the mechanism field explicitly notes that 'no Hungarian evidence confirms any specific alternative format's effectiveness,' meaning the framing choice prioritizes a less-explored lever (exam format) over a more readily implementable one (SES quotas + thresholds) that has moderate evidence cross-nationally, even though both remain weakly evidenced in Hungary specifically.

rejected.framing: Admission-equity regulation limited to state-sector maintainers only, exempting church and foundation institutions
rejected.reason: Rejected because the scenario's goal requires reducing 'family-capital-driven sorting at the point of entry,' but exempting church/foundation maintainers (which now serve 27% of gimnazium students and are growing) would systematically undermine the equity mechanism by channeling excluded low-SES applicants into a two-tier compliance landscape; the scenario acknowledges this as a political risk but correctly treats uniform maintainer coverage as non-negotiable to the mechanism's logic, even though enforcement remains highly uncertain.

scenarios.chosen: Supply-side contraction via formalized score thresholds and capacity caps applied consistently across maintainers

rejected.framing: Demand-side reduction through increased student/family cost-sharing for selective track access (tuition, facility fees)
rejected.reason: Rejected because cost-sharing as a selection mechanism would directly discriminate against lower-SES families and contradict both the scenario's equity framing and European education-access norms; moreover, the evidence record shows that admission gatekeeping (not price) is the primary barrier for disadvantaged pupils, making cost-sharing an inefficient proxy that would reduce equity without materially shrinking capacity.

rejected.framing: Supply-side contraction paired with mandatory compensatory funding for general schools at point of implementation
rejected.reason: Rejected because while compensatory funding would strengthen the equity logic, it would make the scenario's implementation timeline and fiscal feasibility worse than the already-pessimistic assessment documented in the cost_categories field; the scenario explicitly notes no numeric fiscal range is available and consolidation pressure is projected at 6.2% deficit by 2026, making the addition of a mandatory co-requirement unachievable and therefore strategically inferior to the chosen framing, which preserves at least the supply-contraction lever independent of the funding question.

scenarios.chosen: Structural delay of selection age to 14+ via phased abolition of 6- and 8-year gimnazium entry with simultaneous anti-sorting rules for school choice and catchment

rejected.framing: Structural delay of selection age paired with explicit quotas guaranteeing access to delayed-selection tracks for pupils from early elite-track backgrounds, to mitigate top-of-distribution sorting
rejected.reason: Rejected because adding reverse-quotas to protect early-track pupils would directly contradict the equity mechanism (delay works by retaining high-performing pupils in comprehensive settings longer) and would introduce a second, compensatory sorting channel that would recreate stratification at the delayed-selection point; the scenario's mechanism is already load-bearing without this additional framing, which would diffuse accountability and create justiciability problems around quota design.

rejected.framing: Structural delay of selection age implemented sequentially with anti-sorting rules—first delay, then anti-sorting rules introduced after 2-3 year pilot phase
rejected.reason: Rejected because the mechanism field explicitly documents that 'sorting does not simply migrate to other channels only if anti-sorting rules are implemented and enforced simultaneously,' and Finland's post-2000 experience shows that sequential implementation allows sorting to establish itself in school choice and residential channels before enforcement begins; the chosen framing correctly treats simultaneity as a load-bearing requirement, even though it increases implementation complexity, because delayed introduction would allow offsetting behavior to entrench.

scenarios.chosen: Non-structural compensatory reform combining curriculum clarity, statutory-floor TEIP-style targeted funding, and grade-retention reduction

rejected.framing: Non-structural compensatory reform focused exclusively on grade-retention reduction, omitting TEIP-style targeted funding due to budget uncertainty
rejected.reason: Rejected because while grade-retention reduction carries strong evidence (Portugal's EUR 112 million/two-year, 26%-reduction precedent) and is fiscally self-justifying, relying on it alone would abandon the scenario's stated goal of 'equity and achievement gains' and leave the general-school majority without the curriculum-clarity and targeted-support mechanisms documented in the Portugal template; moreover, the retention-reduction lever alone does not address the achievement-gap closure question, which requires the full compensatory package even if TEIP funding remains fiscally uncertain.

rejected.framing: Non-structural compensatory reform explicitly subordinated to structural delay, presented as an interim or supplementary package pending S3 implementation
rejected.reason: Rejected because framing S4 as subordinate or supplementary to S3 would undermine its status as a distinct policy scenario with independent merit and would collapse the decision-maker's ability to evaluate trade-offs between structural and non-structural approaches; moreover, the episodic memory notes that S4's mechanism field acknowledges early selection 'may offset some benefits,' creating a logical interdependence that the scenario correctly preserves as an explicit uncertainty rather than resolving by treating S4 as merely provisional pending S3.

scenarios.chosen: Demographically-driven passive contraction serving as a no-new-policy counterfactual baseline, with reactive consolidation and ongoing monitoring only

rejected.framing: Demographically-driven passive contraction explicitly coupled with documented 'passive' government role, using published KSH statistics to show that policy levers like score thresholds are not operating
rejected.reason: Rejected because the episodic memory and the mechanism field itself note that 'the demography record itself notes government is currently using score thresholds as an additional supply-side lever,' meaning a truly 'passive' baseline does not currently exist; the chosen framing correctly designates S5 as an analytical counterfactual rather than a real-world policy state, which preserves analytic clarity and prevents the scenario from being misinterpreted as a description of current government neutrality.

rejected.framing: Passive contraction with explicit forward-planning for network consolidation, pre-identifying which schools will close and which will persist based on demographic projections
rejected.reason: Rejected because adding explicit consolidation planning would transform S5 from a no-new-policy baseline into a policy scenario with active design choices about which settlements retain secondary provision; the scenario's role is to provide a clean counterfactual against which S1-S4's policy-driven effects can be isolated, and introducing consolidation planning would contaminate that counterfactual and require assigning responsibility for distributional consequences (rural school closures) that the baseline scenario intentionally leaves reactive and unplanned.
